meta.name: Konbini Companion Database
meta.description: Japan convenience store item translator & guide

stores.name: 7-Eleven
stores.tagline: Green stripe, red logo. Open 24/7. The OG konbini.

stores.name: Lawson
stores.tagline: Blue & white. L-kara fried chicken. Famous for Loppi terminal.

stores.name: FamilyMart
stores.tagline: Green & blue. Famichiki chicken. Best crepe cakes.

items.description: Grilled salmon flakes wrapped in seasoned rice with nori seaweed. The classic choice.
items.cultural_note: Onigiri is the original Japanese fast food. The nori seaweed wrapper is usually separated from the rice by a plastic layer — peel it off just before eating to keep it crispy.

items.description: Canned tuna mixed with Japanese mayonnaise wrapped in rice. Comfort food perfection.
items.cultural_note: Japan's mayo (Kewpie) is richer and tangier than Western mayo — made with egg yolks only, no whites. This combo is a national obsession.

items.description: Sour-salty pickled plum (umeboshi) center. Polarizing — you either love it or hate it.
items.cultural_note: Umeboshi is a traditional preservative — samurai would carry it in battle. The extreme sourness is an acquired taste but aids digestion.

items.description: Simmered kelp in sweet soy sauce. Umami bomb, less common outside Japan.
items.cultural_note: Kombu is a foundational ingredient in dashi (Japanese soup stock). Eating it directly like this shows how deeply umami runs in the cuisine.

items.description: Spicy seasoned cod roe — bright orange, slightly spicy, intensely savory.
items.cultural_note: Mentaiko is a Fukuoka specialty. The "spicy" level is mild by international standards — more of a pleasant tingle.

items.description: Small shrimp with mayo. Sweet, creamy, and satisfying — a konbini classic.
items.cultural_note: 7-Eleven's version is particularly beloved. Limited editions (sakura shrimp, garlic shrimp) appear seasonally.

items.description: Chicken and egg simmered in sweet soy broth over rice. 'Parent and child' (chicken + egg).
items.cultural_note: The name is a pun — both the chicken (parent) and egg (child) are in the dish. Don't overthink it, just enjoy.

items.description: Breaded deep-fried pork cutlet with egg and onion sauce over rice. Students eat this before exams for luck.
items.cultural_note: Katsu sounds like 'winning' (勝つ) — eating katsudon before a test or competition is a tradition. 'Katsu!' is also a common cheer.

items.description: Japanese-style pasta — often with mentaiko (cod roe) cream sauce, or Japanese-style meat sauce (napolitan).
items.cultural_note: Japanese 'napolitan' pasta is a retro Western dish with ketchup-based sauce, sausage, and veggies. Very昭和 (Showa-era) comfort food.

items.description: Thick Japanese curry roux with beef/chicken and vegetables over rice. Japanese soul food.
items.cultural_note: Japanese curry is a 'Western' dish — introduced by the British Navy in the Meiji era. It's thicker, sweeter, and milder than Indian curry. The JMSDF still serves curry every Friday.

items.description: Grilled beef or pork with sweet soy glaze, served with rice and pickled veggies.
items.cultural_note: Yakiniku ('grilled meat') is Korean-influenced Japanese BBQ. The sweet-tangy tare sauce is the key — each konbini chain has its own recipe.

items.description: Fresh sushi rolls or sashimi platters. Surprisingly good quality for konbini food.
items.cultural_note: Conbini sushi is a marvel of supply chain logistics — it's made fresh daily and often discounted (半額, hangaku) after 7 PM. Not Japan's best sushi, but better than most 'sushi' abroad.

items.description: Marinated bite-sized chicken, double-fried for maximum crispiness. Famima's is legendary.
items.cultural_note: Lawson's 'L-kara' (Lチキ) and FamilyMart's 'Famichiki' (ファミチキ) have cult followings. Each chain guards their recipe like a state secret. The pre-battered frozen ones you buy are just the beginning — they're fried fresh in-store.

items.description: Fluffy steamed bun filled with savory pork. The quintessential winter konbini snack.
items.cultural_note: The steamers sit by the register in cold months. Variations include pizza-man, curry-man, and sweet an-man (red bean). Ask for 'nikuman hitotsu kudasai' (肉まん一つください).

items.description: Potato and meat cream croquette, breaded and deep-fried. Crispy outside, creamy inside.
items.cultural_note: Korokke is a yoshoku (Western-style) dish from the Meiji era — adapted from French croquettes. Cream corn korokke is a beloved seasonal variant.

items.description: Self-serve hot dog with squirty ketchup and mustard. Pure nostalgia.
items.cultural_note: 7-Eleven's hot dogs are a beloved snack — the bun is steamed soft and fluffy, not toasted. A relic of 80s Japan's fascination with American culture.

items.description: Nissin Cup Noodle — the OG. Shrimp, egg, pork in soy broth. A cultural icon.
items.cultural_note: Invented by Momofuku Ando in 1971. Japan has hundreds of regional and limited-edition flavors. The foil lid isn't just sealing — it's a cooking timer (3 min). Just add hot water from the store's dispenser.

items.description: Creamy seafood broth with crab, scallop, and squid bits. Many say it's the best Cup Noodle flavor.
items.cultural_note: Seafood flavor is Japan-exclusive and has a devoted following. The 'real crab' bits are tiny but the flavor is unmistakable.

items.description: Premium tonkotsu (pork bone broth) ramen from famous Ippudo brand. Restaurant quality at home.
items.cultural_note: Premium cup ramen (¥300+) is a whole different experience — real soup base, better noodles, generous toppings. Look for brands like Ippudo, Afuri, or Nakiryu.

items.description: Thick stir-fried noodles with Worcestershire-style sauce. Add the included sachets of aonori (seaweed) and benishoga (pickled ginger).
items.cultural_note: Not actually soba — yakisoba uses wheat-flour noodles (chuuka-men). The brown sauce is a thick, tangy Worcestershire variant unique to Japan.

items.description: Authentic Japanese green tea in a bottle, no sugar. Ito En Oi Ocha is the gold standard.
items.cultural_note: Japan drinks more green tea from plastic bottles than brewed at home. Ito En's Oi Ocha (伊藤園 お〜いお茶) is the #1 brand. Unlike 'green tea' abroad, this is unsweetened — real tea flavor.

items.description: Toasty, nutty, caffeine-free roasted barley tea. Perfect in summer. Usually served cold.
items.cultural_note: The default summer drink in Japanese homes — many fridges always have a pitcher. It's caffeine-free so kids drink it too. The roasted aroma is pure summer nostalgia for Japanese people.

items.description: Suntory's iconic canned coffee — available hot (from vending machine) or cold. Milk, black, or micro-sugar variants.
items.cultural_note: BOSS is Japan's #1 canned coffee. The famous ad campaign featured Tommy Lee Jones as 'Alien BOSS'. Hot cans are a winter staple from heated vending machines — press the red button for hot, blue for cold.

items.description: Coca-Cola Japan's premium coffee brand. The 'Georgia Max' series is notably good.
items.cultural_note: Japan's canned coffee market is fiercely competitive (BOSS vs Georgia vs Fire vs Roots). Each has 10+ variants. 'Black' (unsweetened) is the health-conscious choice.

items.description: Milky, slightly tart yogurt-based soft drink. An iconic Japanese taste since 1919.
items.cultural_note: The name sounds like 'cow piss' to English ears, hence the 'Calpico' branding abroad. It's actually from 'calcium' + 'sap' (sanskrit for taste). Dilute concentrate 3:1 with water (or milk for 'Calpis Soda').

items.description: Japanese lemon-lime soda in a Codd-neck bottle with a marble. The summer festival drink.
items.cultural_note: The marble stopper is a gimmick — you push it down into the bottle to open. Don't lose the plastic ring! Try original or yuzu flavor. Not usually at konbini but at festivals (matsuri) and souvenir shops. In konbini, buy the plastic bottle version.

items.description: Creamy sweet matcha and milk blend. The canned/bottle versions from konbini are surprisingly good.
items.cultural_note: Matcha lattes became a global phenomenon, but in Japan they're a konbini staple. Brands like Kirin's 'Gogo no Koucha' and Suntory's 'Iyemon' make excellent versions.

items.description: 9% ABV canned citrus cocktail. The legendary drink that sneaks up on you. Double Lemon is the classic.
items.cultural_note: Strong Zero is a rite of passage. At 9% ABV (double a typical beer) in a tall can, it's dangerously smooth. The 'zero' means zero sugar, not zero alcohol. Popularized by street drinkers in Shibuya and park gatherings. Respect the can — it's stronger than it tastes.

items.description: Lemon-flavored shochu highball. Refreshing, lower alcohol (3-5%), perfect with food.
items.cultural_note: Chu-Hi = shochu + highball. The standard lemon flavor is the baseline — every izakaya has their own take. Konbini versions by Kirin and Suntory are excellent.

items.description: Japan's oldest beer brand (1876). Crisp, clean lager. The gold standard of Japanese beer.
items.cultural_note: Sapporo is the 'original'. Look for 'Sapporo Premium' (black label with red star) or 'Ebisu' (Yebisu) — the premium version. 'Happoshu' (発泡酒) and 'Dai-san no biiru' (第三のビール) are cheaper malt alternatives if you're on a budget.

items.description: Sweet, fragrant Japanese plum liqueur. Sip over ice (roku) or with soda (soda-wari).
items.cultural_note: Umeshu is made by steeping ume (Japanese plums) in shochu and sugar. Home-brewing is common — families often have a jar steeping. Konbini sells cute single-serve cans perfect for hotel room sipping.

items.description: Cheaper beer alternative with less malt. Lighter taste, lighter wallet impact.
items.cultural_note: Happoshu was invented to dodge Japan's high malt tax. The 'third beer' (第三のビール) category uses soy protein or pea protein instead of malt — even cheaper and surprisingly not terrible. Popular brands: Hon Kirin, Tanrei.

items.description: Crunchy crescent-shaped rice crackers with a spicy kick. Often mixed with peanuts.
items.cultural_note: The name means 'persimmon seeds' (they look like seeds). The spicy-savory combo is addictive. Mixed with peanuts it's called 'kaki-pi' — THE classic beer snack.

items.jp: わさびビーフジャーキー
items.description: Beef jerky with real wasabi kick. Surprisingly high quality, excellent with beer.
items.cultural_note: Lawson's wasabi beef jerky has a cult following. The wasabi is real — the heat hits your nose, not your tongue. A fantastic omiyage (souvenir) to bring home.

items.description: Crunchy, thick potato sticks in a cup — salad (green) and cheese (yellow) are classics.
items.cultural_note: Jagariko has a distinctive trapezoid cup shape and comes with a tiny fork. Limited edition flavors (consomme, teriyaki, butter soy sauce) drop regularly and sell out fast.

items.description: Pretzel sticks dipped in chocolate. Japan's most famous exported snack. Try matcha or strawberry limited editions.
items.cultural_note: Pocky Day (11/11) is a real Japanese holiday — the sticks look like 1s. Glico releases special flavors just for this day. Pocky has dozens of Japan-exclusive flavors: purple sweet potato, mango, crème brûlée.

items.description: Japan has 300+ KitKat flavors — matcha, sake, sweet potato, melon, cheesecake, and more.
items.cultural_note: KitKat in Japan is associated with good luck — 'Kitto Katsu' (きっと勝つ = 'surely win') sounds like the brand name. Students eat them before exams. Regional exclusives (Shinshu apple, Kyushu sweet potato) are popular omiyage.

items.description: Crispy fried kelp chips — umami-rich and surprisingly light. Healthy-ish by snack standards.
items.cultural_note: These are often found in the 'otokuyouhin' (value bin) section. They're a Hokkaido specialty but available nationwide at most konbini.

items.description: Cylindrical puffed corn snack, cheese flavor. Iconic cheap snack at ¥10 each. Mental note: price is rising to ¥12 in 2025.
items.cultural_note: Umaibo has held the ¥10 price point since 1979 — a miracle of Japanese deflation economics. The recent price hike to ¥12 (2025) made national news. There's even a Umaibo museum in Tochigi.

items.description: Silky egg custard with caramel sauce. A konbini staple that beats most restaurant versions.
items.cultural_note: Japanese pudding is closer to French crème caramel than American pudding. The caramel is usually a liquid layer on top. Tofu-like texture when done right. 7-Eleven's is considered the gold standard among budget purins.

items.description: Layers of thin crepes with whipped cream and fruit. Light, elegant, dangerously good.
items.cultural_note: FamilyMart's crepe cakes are particularly good. The strawberry shortcake crepe is a seasonal highlight during spring. This is the kind of thing that convinces people Japanese konbini food is unmatched.

items.description: Chewy rice dough wrapping vanilla/matcha ice cream. Bite-sized bliss.
items.cultural_note: Lotte's Yukimi Daifuku (雪見だいふく) is the OG — 'snow-viewing daifuku'. The mochi skin stays chewy even frozen. Don't microwave (it melts the ice cream inside) — let it sit 2-3 min at room temp.

items.description: Traditional Japanese dessert with agar jelly, sweet bean paste, fruit, and black sugar syrup.
items.cultural_note: Anmitsu is a Meiji-era creation. The black sugar syrup (kuromitsu) is the key — pour it over everything. Konbini versions come in cute compartmented cups.

items.description: Creamy soft-serve with chocolate dip. Always hits the spot on a humid day.
items.cultural_note: 7-Eleven's soft serve machine is legendary. The 'cremia' (クレミア) soft serve — available at some konbini — has 12.5% butterfat content, making it incredibly rich.

items.description: Two small pancake-like patties sandwiching sweet red bean paste. Doraemon's favorite food.
items.cultural_note: Dorayaki is famous worldwide as Doraemon's favorite snack. The best version has a thin layer of mochi-like texture. Try the 'shiro-an' (white bean) variant if you see it.

items.description: Clear plastic umbrella — the savior when sudden rain catches you. Sold at every konbini worldwide, but in Japan they're practically a uniform.
items.cultural_note: Clear umbrellas are the Japanese default because they don't obscure vision on crowded streets. When it rains in Tokyo, every konbini puts a basket of them by the entrance. You'll see the city transformed into a sea of clear domes.

items.description: Individually packaged dress shirts in your size — laundry-fresh. Yes, really, at a convenience store.
items.cultural_note: A uniquely Japanese konbini service — salarymen who had a long night and need a fresh shirt for the morning grab these. They come laundered, starched, and individually packaged.

items.description: Compact toothbrush + mini toothpaste. What to grab when your hotel doesn't provide one (or you forgot yours).
items.cultural_note: Many Japanese hotels don't provide toiletries by default (eco policy). The toothbrush sets near the konbini register are a traveler's lifeline. They also sell travel-sized everything — shampoo, razor, contact lens solution.

items.description: Small pocket pack of tissues. Often given away for free by advertisers, but konbini sells them too.
items.cultural_note: Free tissue packs are a classic Japanese advertising medium — handed out in front of stations by people in costumes. They're used ONCE here (blow, fold, discard) because they're practically free.

items.description: Prepaid data SIM for tourists. Available at bigger konbini (especially near airports and stations).
items.cultural_note: Look for 'Japan Travel SIM' by NTT Docomo or Softbank. 7-Eleven also sells their own '7-11 Talk & Data SIM'. You'll need to show your passport to buy — it's for tourist use only.

items.description: Postage stamps for sending postcards and letters. Standard postcard rate to overseas is ¥70.
items.cultural_note: The konbini clerk can weigh your mail and sell you the correct stamps. Postcards to overseas: ¥70 (air). Letters start at ¥120 for up to 25g. Also sell cute character-themed washi tapes and stationery.

items.description: Single-use packs of shampoo, conditioner, body soap. Perfect for capsule hotels or hostels.
items.cultural_note: These tiny sachets are a lifesaver at shared showers. Tsubaki (椿) and Pantene are the most common brands. The konbini also sells 'dryers' (coin-operated in some locations).

items.description: International ATM accepting foreign cards (Visa, Mastercard, etc.) for cash withdrawal.
items.cultural_note: 7-Eleven ATMs are the best for international cards — lower fees, English UI, and available 24/7 at 21,000+ stores. Lawson ATMs (beyond) and FamilyMart ATMs (E-net) also work but may charge higher fees. Japan is still VERY cash-based.

items.description: Full color copier/printer/scanner. Accepts USB, SD card, and smartphone transfer (app or QR).
items.cultural_note: A lifesaver for printing tickets, boarding passes, or documents. The interface is available in English (press 'English' at the top). You can also print photos from your phone — great for instant Shutterstock-type souvenir prints.

items.description: Pick up pre-booked event tickets (concerts, films, Shinkansen, theme parks) at the terminal.
items.cultural_note: Lawson's 'Loppi' (ロッピー) and FamilyMart's 'Fami Port' (ファミポート) are essential for buying/reserving event tickets, concert tickets (Ticket Pia), Ghibli Museum passes, and highway bus seats. Many online orders in Japan require konbini payment/pickup.

items.description: Pay utility bills, taxes, insurance, and online shopping invoices at the register.
items.cultural_note: Paying bills at konbini is still common practice in Japan — young people do it more than you'd expect. Just take the barcode slip to the register and they'll scan and collect payment. Receipt is your proof of payment.

items.description: Ship luggage to your next hotel or the airport. A game-changer for traveling Japan.
items.cultural_note: Konbini takkyuubin (宅配便) is a game-changer. Drop your luggage at 7-Eleven (Yamato/Kuroneko) or FamilyMart and it arrives at your next hotel the next day. You travel hands-free. Fill out the form at the counter, pay, and done. Omiyato (souvenir shipping home) also available.
